dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: Assertions are mostly correct and properly sourced from the census table. However, name/age/birthplace for all household members should have informant_proximity 'household_member', but residence assertions correctly use 'witness' for the enumerator. The critical issue: three children (Thomas, Bridget, John) have no occupation column entries in the source data, yet Claude correctly did not create occupation assertions for them—only Patrick has 'Laborer' listed. Birth year calculations (1850 minus stated age) are arithmetically sound. However, the skill notes that subject I1 (Patrick Flynn born ca. 1845 PA) is a distinct individual from head Patrick (age 32, born ~1818), which is correct reasoning but represents a negative finding—this record does not help identify the subject's parents. The extraction itself is factually accurate to the source.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted assertions for all five persons in the household (Patrick, Mary, Thomas, Bridget, John). For each person, it captured: name, age, birthplace, residence, and relationship (where applicable). Occupation was correctly extracted only for Patrick (the only person with 'Laborer' listed). Birth year assertions were inferred from age. The skill also correctly identified that the subject (I1) does not appear in this record as a child, explaining why no sibling-stub trigger occurred. All household members relevant to the research FAN network were included.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: Both MCP tool calls executed successfully with matched.kind='live'. The tree_edit call added the source with correct title, author, and FamilySearch URL. The research_append call submitted 30 assertion entries (one source + 29 assertions: 6 for head Patrick, 6 for wife Mary, 6 for child_1 Thomas, 6 for child_2 Bridget, 5 for child_3 John—with all relationship assertions correctly marked). No fixture_not_found errors. Arguments match the expected structure and content.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Every assertion is a single extractable fact. Name, age, birth year, birthplace, occupation, residence, and relationship are each in separate assertions with distinct fact_type values. For example, Patrick Flynn's information is decomposed into: name (a_001), age (a_002), birth year (a_004), birthplace (a_005), occupation (a_006), residence (a_007), and relationship (implicit from record_role). No compound assertions combining, e.g., 'name and birthplace' or 'age and birth year' into one value field.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: Informant field correctly identifies the actual informant rather than just 'census'. For name/age/birthplace of adults and children, informant is 'unknown household member' with reasoning: 'likely self or spouse' for Patrick and Mary; 'likely a parent' for Thomas, Bridget, John. informant_proximity correctly distinguishes: 'household_member' for facts reported by household members; 'witness' for residence (enumerator observed the dwelling). informant_bias_notes explains the reasoning in each case. Census enumerator is correctly identified as the recorder, not the informant for personal facts.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Evidence types are correctly assigned per the 1850 census structure (no explicit relationship column until 1880). Direct evidence: name, age, birthplace, occupation, residence—all explicitly stated in the census table. Indirect evidence: birth year (calculated from age, marked as such with date_certainty='calculated') and all relationship assertions (inferred from household position since no relationship column exists in 1850). No negative evidence needed (no absences marked). The skill correctly recognizes that 1850 co-residence relationships are indirect, not direct.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Correctness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Claude extracted all three individuals' factual data correctly from the provided census record. The 21 assertions accurately reflect the stated facts (names, ages, birthplace Ireland, occupation miner, gender, residence). Birth years were correctly computed as indirect evidence via age arithmetic. The skill properly distinguished between direct evidence (stated facts like age, birthplace) and indirect evidence (computed birth year, inferred relationships). No fabricated material; all outputs supported by the source.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Claude addressed every element required: extracted assertions for all three people (Thomas, Mary, Patrick), covered all available facts (name, age, birthplace, occupation for Thomas; name, age, birthplace for Mary and Patrick), correctly classified occupation only for Thomas (not fabricating for Mary), created log entry, added source to both research.json and tree.gedcomx.json, and generated 21 atomic assertions as specified. No silent omissions.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All MCP tool calls passed arguments correctly. research_log_append was called with appropriate query parameters (record type, county, state, household head). tree_edit add_source was called with title and author. research_append operations correctly included section, op, and entry fields with proper assertion structure. All calls succeeded (matched.kind == 'live'); second research_append attempt was preceded by tree_edit to ensure S1 existed before referencing it. No fixture_not_found errors.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Each assertion is a single extractable fact. Claude created separate assertions for each person's name, age, birth year, birthplace, occupation (Thomas only), gender, residence, and relationships. For example, Patrick Flynn is decomposed into 7 separate assertions: name, age, birth year, birthplace, gender, residence, relationship—not compound claims like 'Patrick Flynn, age 5, born Ireland' in one value. Downstream skills can query individual facts.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Informant identification
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Informants are mostly well-identified but with some generic proximity assignments. Claude correctly distinguished census enumerator (witness, gender/residence) from household members (likely Thomas or Mary for adult facts, likely a parent for Patrick's facts). However, for adult name, age, birthplace assertions, the skill uses 'unknown household member (likely self or spouse)' with 'household_member' proximity—reasonable but not distinguishing whether the informant was the person themselves or someone else. For Patrick's facts, 'likely a parent' is appropriately specific. Relationship assertions correctly identify enumerator as witness.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Evidence types are correctly assigned throughout. Direct evidence: stated facts (age 32, age 28, age 5, birthplace Ireland, occupation miner, gender, names) are all marked direct. Indirect evidence: computed birth years (~1818, ~1822, ~1845 derived from age minus 1850) are correctly marked indirect. Relationship assertions (Mary as wife, Patrick as child) are correctly marked indirect because the 1850 census has no relationship column—relationships are inferred from household position alone, not from explicit statements. Enumerator-observed facts (gender, residence) are direct with witness proximity. All assignments align with source content.

output.text_response:
**Read complete.** Project: identify parents of Patrick Flynn (I1), born ca. 1845 in Schuylkill County PA. No sources or assertions yet → first source will be `src_001`, tree has only I1.

**Plan:**
- Roles: `head_of_household` (Thomas), `wife` (Mary), `child_1` (Patrick = I1)
- 21 assertions: name, age, birth year (indirect), birthplace, occupation/gender/residence per person, plus inferred relationships for Mary and Patrick
- Record is user-provided → log entry first, then batch source + assertions, then tree `S1`
- No other children → no sibling stubs needed

**Step 4 — log entry first:****Step 5a/5b — batch source + 21 assertions:**The schema requires top-level `section` and `op` even when using `ops`. Let me retry with those included:Need to write `S1` to the tree first, then the research batch. 

**Step 5c — tree source first:**`S1` assigned. Now batch source + 21 assertions:All 22 ops committed. Here's the summary:

---

**Extraction complete — `src_001` / `S1`**

- **Source:** `src_001` (original) — 1850 U.S. Federal Census, Schuylkill County, PA, Thomas Flynn household; tree description `S1`
- **Assertions:** 21 total — `head_of_household` ×7 (Thomas: name, age, birth ~1818, birthplace, occupation, gender, residence); `wife` ×7 (Mary: name, age, birth ~1822, birthplace, gender, residence, relationship-inferred); `child_1` ×7 (Patrick: name, age, birth ~1845, birthplace, gender, residence, relationship-inferred)
- **Tree changes:** `S1` added; no sibling stubs (Patrick is the only child in the household)
- **⚠ Key conflict:** `child_1` birthplace recorded as **Ireland**, not Pennsylvania — contradicts the project objective's "born ca. 1845 in Pennsylvania." This needs conflict-resolution or further corroboration.
- **Pre-1880 note:** All three relationship assertions (`a_014`, `a_021`) are `indirect` — the 1850 census has no relationship column; both are position-inferred.
- **Next:** Run `check-warnings` for plausibility checks, then `person-evidence` to link `child_1` (Patrick Flynn, age 5) to I1.

args.notes: User-provided transcription: Thomas Flynn, age 32, male, born Ireland, miner; Mary Flynn, age 28, female, born Ireland; Patrick Flynn, age 5, male, born Ireland. Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: Claude extracted all three individuals' factual data correctly from the provided census record. The 21 assertions accurately reflect the stated facts (names, ages, birthplace Ireland, occupation miner, gender, residence). Birth years were correctly computed as indirect evidence via age arithmetic. The skill properly distinguished between direct evidence (stated facts like age, birthplace) and indirect evidence (computed birth year, inferred relationships). No fabricated material; all outputs supported by the source.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: Claude addressed every element required: extracted assertions for all three people (Thomas, Mary, Patrick), covered all available facts (name, age, birthplace, occupation for Thomas; name, age, birthplace for Mary and Patrick), correctly classified occupation only for Thomas (not fabricating for Mary), created log entry, added source to both research.json and tree.gedcomx.json, and generated 21 atomic assertions as specified. No silent omissions.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: All MCP tool calls passed arguments correctly. research_log_append was called with appropriate query parameters (record type, county, state, household head). tree_edit add_source was called with title and author. research_append operations correctly included section, op, and entry fields with proper assertion structure. All calls succeeded (matched.kind == 'live'); second research_append attempt was preceded by tree_edit to ensure S1 existed before referencing it. No fixture_not_found errors.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Each assertion is a single extractable fact. Claude created separate assertions for each person's name, age, birth year, birthplace, occupation (Thomas only), gender, residence, and relationships. For example, Patrick Flynn is decomposed into 7 separate assertions: name, age, birth year, birthplace, gender, residence, relationship—not compound claims like 'Patrick Flynn, age 5, born Ireland' in one value. Downstream skills can query individual facts.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: Informants are mostly well-identified but with some generic proximity assignments. Claude correctly distinguished census enumerator (witness, gender/residence) from household members (likely Thomas or Mary for adult facts, likely a parent for Patrick's facts). However, for adult name, age, birthplace assertions, the skill uses 'unknown household member (likely self or spouse)' with 'household_member' proximity—reasonable but not distinguishing whether the informant was the person themselves or someone else. For Patrick's facts, 'likely a parent' is appropriately specific. Relationship assertions correctly identify enumerator as witness.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Evidence types are correctly assigned throughout. Direct evidence: stated facts (age 32, age 28, age 5, birthplace Ireland, occupation miner, gender, names) are all marked direct. Indirect evidence: computed birth years (~1818, ~1822, ~1845 derived from age minus 1850) are correctly marked indirect. Relationship assertions (Mary as wife, Patrick as child) are correctly marked indirect because the 1850 census has no relationship column—relationships are inferred from household position alone, not from explicit statements. Enumerator-observed facts (gender, residence) are direct with witness proximity. All assignments align with source content.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Correctness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All assertions were extracted correctly from the census record with proper fact types, informants, and evidence types. Patrick's missing birthplace assertion was properly added as a separate atomic claim. Thomas's facts were correctly identified and extracted. Bridget and John's assertions accurately reflect the source data. The skill correctly identified Patrick's existing status (I1) and did not duplicate him. Person stubs for Bridget and John were created with proper IDs (I5, I6) as assigned by tree_edit. ParentChild relationships were correctly created (R4, R5) linking both siblings to Thomas (I2). No fabricated material or contradictions present.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill addressed all requirements. Every household member (Thomas, Bridget, Patrick, John) had assertions extracted for name, age/birth, and birthplace at minimum. The record_role corrections were completed (a_001–a_004 from child_1 to child_2; a_005 evidence_type from indirect to direct). Person stubs were created for the two siblings not already in tree (Bridget I5, John I6). ParentChild edges were established for both new siblings. Existing persons (Mary I3, James I4) and relationships (R1, R2, R3) were correctly skipped. The source URL was updated to the full ARK. No silent omissions detected.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Most MCP calls had correct arguments and succeeded. The research_append calls for assertions (a_014 through a_031) all passed with properly structured entries including source_id, record_id, record_role, fact_type, value, informant, evidence_type, and other fields. The tree_edit calls correctly added two person stubs with gender and birth name, and correctly added two ParentChild relationships. However, there were two failed append attempts at the beginning (attempting to append placeholder entries to sources and assertions sections without required fields), which represent noise even though they did not ultimately prevent the successful extractions. The skill recovered and made correct subsequent calls, but these initial missteps indicate partial execution efficiency.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Every assertion extracted is a single, atomic fact. Name, age, birth year (computed), birthplace, occupation, residence, and relationship are each tracked as separate assertions with their own fact_type. For example, Bridget's information is decomposed into a_020 (name), a_021 (age), a_022 (birth year), a_023 (birthplace), a_024 (residence), and a_025 (relationship). Patrick's missing birthplace is appropriately split from his existing age assertion. Thomas's facts span multiple fact_types (age, birth, birthplace, occupation, residence). No compound claims were bundled into single assertions. Downstream skills can query individual fact_type values without disambiguation.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Informant identification
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Informants were consistently identified with proper specificity and proximity assessed. For household member facts (name, age, birthplace), informant was identified as 'Unknown household member (likely a parent)' with informant_proximity marked as 'household_member'. For Thomas's facts, informant was 'Unknown household member (likely Thomas Flynn himself or spouse)' also with 'household_member' proximity. For residence facts, the census enumerator was correctly identified as the informant with informant_proximity 'witness' (they visited the dwelling). Relationship facts were marked as inferred from household structure with informant_proximity 'unknown' because 1850 census had no explicit relationship column. The skill distinguished between recorder and actual informant appropriately.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Evidence types were correctly assigned throughout. Direct evidence was used for explicitly stated facts in the census: name (stated), age (stated), birthplace (stated), occupation (stated), residence (stated by enumerator observation). Indirect evidence was used for computed birth years derived by subtracting stated ages from the 1850 census year, and for relationship inferences based on household position (1850 census had no explicit relationship column—that was not introduced until 1880). The distinction between direct household member reporting and indirect inference is consistently applied. All classifications support proper downstream conflict resolution.

output.text_response:
**Pre-write enumeration:**
- Existing `src_001` / `S1` covers this record (dwelling 84, family 91) — reuse, update URL to full ARK.
- Existing assertions `a_001–a_004` have `record_role: child_1` for Patrick, but Bridget (age 7) is the first-listed child → correct to `child_2`. Also `a_005` has `evidence_type: indirect` for Thomas's name — correct to `direct`.
- Parents in tree: Thomas Flynn = **I2** ✓ (trigger fires); no wife listed in record.
- Siblings to check: Bridget Flynn (F) → not in tree; John Flynn (M) → not in tree. Both need stubs.
- Mary Flynn (I3) and James Flynn (I4) are in the tree but not from this record — leave untouched.Now the large batched call — 5 updates + 18 appends in one shot:The `ops` batch isn't supported by `research_append`'s schema. Making individual calls — updates first, then appends.Now the 18 appends — Patrick's missing birthplace first:All 18 appends done. Now tree batch 1 — add Bridget and John as person stubs (S1 already exists):Bridget = **I5**, John = **I6**. Now tree batch 2 — ParentChild edges from Thomas (I2):All writes confirmed. Here's the terse summary:

**Source:** `src_001` / `S1` reused — URL updated to full ARK `ark:/61903/1:1:MXYZ-TP4`.

**Updates (6):** `a_001–a_004` `record_role` corrected `child_1` → `child_2` (Bridget is the first-listed child); `a_005` `evidence_type` corrected `indirect` → `direct`.

**New assertions (18):**
- `child_2` Patrick — `a_014`: birthplace Ireland (separate from existing age assertion)
- `head_of_household` Thomas — `a_015–a_019`: age 32, birth year ~1818, birthplace Ireland, occupation Laborer, residence Schuylkill Co.
- `child_1` Bridget — `a_020–a_025`: name, age 7, birth year ~1843, birthplace Ireland, residence, relationship
- `child_3` John — `a_026–a_031`: name, age 2, birth year ~1848, birthplace Pennsylvania, residence, relationship

**Tree:** Person stubs added — Bridget Flynn `I5` (F), John Flynn `I6` (M). ParentChild edges R4 (I2→I5) and R5 (I2→I6).

**Key findings:**
- No wife/mother enumerated — Thomas's household in 1850 has no spouse; possible widowhood or temporary absence worth flagging.
- Emigration window: Bridget (~1843) and Patrick (~1845) born Ireland; John (~1848) born Pennsylvania → family arrived in Schuylkill Co. between ~1845 and ~1848.

**Next:** run `check-warnings` for plausibility checks, then `person-evidence` to link Bridget (I5) and John (I6) to their new assertions.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: All assertions were extracted correctly from the census record with proper fact types, informants, and evidence types. Patrick's missing birthplace assertion was properly added as a separate atomic claim. Thomas's facts were correctly identified and extracted. Bridget and John's assertions accurately reflect the source data. The skill correctly identified Patrick's existing status (I1) and did not duplicate him. Person stubs for Bridget and John were created with proper IDs (I5, I6) as assigned by tree_edit. ParentChild relationships were correctly created (R4, R5) linking both siblings to Thomas (I2). No fabricated material or contradictions present.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill addressed all requirements. Every household member (Thomas, Bridget, Patrick, John) had assertions extracted for name, age/birth, and birthplace at minimum. The record_role corrections were completed (a_001–a_004 from child_1 to child_2; a_005 evidence_type from indirect to direct). Person stubs were created for the two siblings not already in tree (Bridget I5, John I6). ParentChild edges were established for both new siblings. Existing persons (Mary I3, James I4) and relationships (R1, R2, R3) were correctly skipped. The source URL was updated to the full ARK. No silent omissions detected.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: Most MCP calls had correct arguments and succeeded. The research_append calls for assertions (a_014 through a_031) all passed with properly structured entries including source_id, record_id, record_role, fact_type, value, informant, evidence_type, and other fields. The tree_edit calls correctly added two person stubs with gender and birth name, and correctly added two ParentChild relationships. However, there were two failed append attempts at the beginning (attempting to append placeholder entries to sources and assertions sections without required fields), which represent noise even though they did not ultimately prevent the successful extractions. The skill recovered and made correct subsequent calls, but these initial missteps indicate partial execution efficiency.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Every assertion extracted is a single, atomic fact. Name, age, birth year (computed), birthplace, occupation, residence, and relationship are each tracked as separate assertions with their own fact_type. For example, Bridget's information is decomposed into a_020 (name), a_021 (age), a_022 (birth year), a_023 (birthplace), a_024 (residence), and a_025 (relationship). Patrick's missing birthplace is appropriately split from his existing age assertion. Thomas's facts span multiple fact_types (age, birth, birthplace, occupation, residence). No compound claims were bundled into single assertions. Downstream skills can query individual fact_type values without disambiguation.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: Informants were consistently identified with proper specificity and proximity assessed. For household member facts (name, age, birthplace), informant was identified as 'Unknown household member (likely a parent)' with informant_proximity marked as 'household_member'. For Thomas's facts, informant was 'Unknown household member (likely Thomas Flynn himself or spouse)' also with 'household_member' proximity. For residence facts, the census enumerator was correctly identified as the informant with informant_proximity 'witness' (they visited the dwelling). Relationship facts were marked as inferred from household structure with informant_proximity 'unknown' because 1850 census had no explicit relationship column. The skill distinguished between recorder and actual informant appropriately.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Evidence types were correctly assigned throughout. Direct evidence was used for explicitly stated facts in the census: name (stated), age (stated), birthplace (stated), occupation (stated), residence (stated by enumerator observation). Indirect evidence was used for computed birth years derived by subtracting stated ages from the 1850 census year, and for relationship inferences based on household position (1850 census had no explicit relationship column—that was not introduced until 1880). The distinction between direct household member reporting and indirect inference is consistently applied. All classifications support proper downstream conflict resolution.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Correctness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Overall extracted facts are factually correct and well-sourced from the death certificate. However, there is one incorrect evidence_type assignment: birthplace (Ireland) for Patrick is marked as 'direct' evidence when it should be 'indirect'—the informant Mary Flynn did not witness Patrick's birth in Ireland; she learned this secondhand. The skill correctly identifies most informant/proximity relationships and appropriately marks parents' information as indirect, but this single misclassification of Patrick's birthplace undermines evidence integrity.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted all essential facts from the death certificate: name, sex, race, age, calculated birth year, birthplace, occupation, death date, death place, cause of death, illness duration, burial date/place, parents' names and birthplaces, and informant details. All major fields from the certificate are represented as separate atomic assertions. No significant information was omitted.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All MCP tool calls used correct arguments. Log append call properly structured query with certificate details. Tree edit call correctly added source without expecting prior existence. Research append calls properly referenced source ID 'S1' after tree edit confirmed it, with appropriate ops arrays containing all assertion entries. Tool usage was methodical and semantically correct across all four function invocations.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Each assertion represents a single extractable fact. Death date (March 12, 1908) and death place (Shenandoah, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania) are separate assertions; age and calculated birth year are separate; parents' names and birthplaces are split into individual assertions. Compound information is properly decomposed, enabling downstream skills to query individual facts without extracting multiple facts from one value field.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Informant identification
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Informant identification is precise throughout. Mary Flynn is explicitly named for biographical facts (name, sex, race, occupation) with 'witness' proximity; Dr. William H. Stein (physician) is correctly identified for death date, place, cause, and illness duration with 'official_duty' proximity; J.J. Franey (undertaker) is named for burial facts with 'official_duty' proximity. Parents' names and Patrick's birthplace are all attributed to Mary Flynn with 'family_not_present' proximity, correctly distinguishing that she reported secondhand information. Informant proximity is nuanced and contextually accurate across all assertions.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Most evidence types are correctly assigned. Death date, death place, cause of death, and burial information are appropriately marked 'direct' as reported by officials/informants present. Parents' names and birthplaces are correctly marked 'indirect' as hearsay. Calculated birth year is correctly marked 'indirect.' However, Patrick's birthplace (Ireland) is incorrectly marked as 'direct' evidence when it should be 'indirect'—Mary Flynn reported it secondhand, not from personal witness. The guidance explicitly states birth-related facts from the informant who was not present are indirect evidence. This single misclassification is the main error; otherwise evidence type assignments align with source content and proximity.

output.text_response:
Now I have the full project state. Let me plan before writing:

- **Source:** `src_001` (first source), `S1` (first GedcomX source desc)
- **Record ID:** `capture:pa-death-certificate-1908-file-89072`
- **Classification:** derivative (death cert records informant testimony about birth, parents, etc.)
- **Roles:** `deceased` (Patrick Flynn), `father_of_deceased` (Thomas Flynn), `mother_of_deceased` (Mary Brennan), `informant` (Mary Flynn)
- **Informants:** Dr. Stein → cause of death / death date+place (`official_duty`); J.J. Franey → burial (`official_duty`); Mary Flynn → biographical/identifying facts (`witness` for name/occupation/sex she observed; `family_not_present` for birth/parents she didn't witness)
- **Parents' name + birthplace:** `indirect` evidence per death-cert guidance
- **Sibling stubs:** not triggered — Patrick is `deceased`, not `child_N`

Step 1: log entry.Step 2: batch source + all 20 assertions.I need to add the GedcomX source description to `tree.gedcomx.json` first, then the `research_append` can reference it.`S1` written. Now the full batch:All written. Here's the summary:

---

**Source:** `src_001` / `S1` — PA Certificate of Death, Patrick Flynn, 1908 (derivative)

**Assertions:** 20 total
- `deceased` (Patrick Flynn): 14 — name, sex, race, age, birth ~1845 (calculated, indirect), birthplace Ireland, occupation, death date, death place, cause of death, illness duration, burial date, burial place
- `father_of_deceased` (Thomas Flynn): 2 — name, birthplace Ireland (both **indirect**)
- `mother_of_deceased` (Mary Brennan): 2 — maiden name, birthplace Ireland (both **indirect**)
- `informant` (Mary Flynn): 3 — name, relationship (wife), residence 214 Main St Shenandoah PA

**Tree changes:** `S1` added; no person stubs (sibling-stub trigger does not apply — Patrick is `deceased`, not `child_N`)

**Key findings for research objective:** Parents named as **Thomas Flynn** (b. Ireland) and **Mary Brennan** (b. Ireland, maiden name) — critical indirect evidence; informant (wife Mary Flynn) did not witness these events; treat as leads requiring corroboration. Birth year ~1845 contradicts the project objective's "born ca. 1845 in Pennsylvania" — the cert says **Ireland**, not Pennsylvania.

**Next steps:** Run `check-warnings` for plausibility issues, then `person-evidence` to link `a_001`–`a_020` to I1 and create person stubs for Thomas Flynn and Mary Brennan.

added.value: ~1845 (calculated from age 63 years, 2 months, 10 days at death 12 March 1908)
added.informant_bias_notes: Informant did not witness decedent's birth; birth year derived by calculation from stated age and death date.
Informant did not witness decedent's birth in Ireland; she learned this from the decedent or family knowledge.
Per GPS practice, parents' names on a death certificate are indirect evidence — the informant reports what she was told about the decedent's father, not a fact she personally witnessed.
Informant did not witness father's birthplace; secondary knowledge transmitted through the decedent or family oral tradition.

Maiden name of decedent's mother. Per GPS practice, parents' names on a death certificate are indirect evidence — informant reports what she was told, not a fact she witnessed. Surname Brennan is the mother's pre-marriage name.
Informant did not witness mother's birthplace; secondary knowledge transmitted through the decedent or family oral tradition.

added.notes: User provided Pennsylvania Certificate of Death for Patrick Flynn, File No. 89072, Registration District 2436, died 12 March 1908, Shenandoah, Schuylkill County, PA. Informant: Mary Flynn (wife). Attending physician: Dr. William H. Stein.

warnings.advisory: Skill called MCP tools but the rubric has no dimension name suggesting tool-usage coverage (matched against keywords: ['tool usage', 'tool use', 'tool work', 'tool call', 'argument quality', 'argument-quality', 'response interpretation', 'tool selection', 'mcp tool', 'fixture']). The judge will grade other dimensions but won't score tool work explicitly. Consider adding a tool-usage dimension or renaming an existing one.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: Overall extracted facts are factually correct and well-sourced from the death certificate. However, there is one incorrect evidence_type assignment: birthplace (Ireland) for Patrick is marked as 'direct' evidence when it should be 'indirect'—the informant Mary Flynn did not witness Patrick's birth in Ireland; she learned this secondhand. The skill correctly identifies most informant/proximity relationships and appropriately marks parents' information as indirect, but this single misclassification of Patrick's birthplace undermines evidence integrity.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted all essential facts from the death certificate: name, sex, race, age, calculated birth year, birthplace, occupation, death date, death place, cause of death, illness duration, burial date/place, parents' names and birthplaces, and informant details. All major fields from the certificate are represented as separate atomic assertions. No significant information was omitted.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: All MCP tool calls used correct arguments. Log append call properly structured query with certificate details. Tree edit call correctly added source without expecting prior existence. Research append calls properly referenced source ID 'S1' after tree edit confirmed it, with appropriate ops arrays containing all assertion entries. Tool usage was methodical and semantically correct across all four function invocations.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Each assertion represents a single extractable fact. Death date (March 12, 1908) and death place (Shenandoah, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania) are separate assertions; age and calculated birth year are separate; parents' names and birthplaces are split into individual assertions. Compound information is properly decomposed, enabling downstream skills to query individual facts without extracting multiple facts from one value field.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: Informant identification is precise throughout. Mary Flynn is explicitly named for biographical facts (name, sex, race, occupation) with 'witness' proximity; Dr. William H. Stein (physician) is correctly identified for death date, place, cause, and illness duration with 'official_duty' proximity; J.J. Franey (undertaker) is named for burial facts with 'official_duty' proximity. Parents' names and Patrick's birthplace are all attributed to Mary Flynn with 'family_not_present' proximity, correctly distinguishing that she reported secondhand information. Informant proximity is nuanced and contextually accurate across all assertions.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Most evidence types are correctly assigned. Death date, death place, cause of death, and burial information are appropriately marked 'direct' as reported by officials/informants present. Parents' names and birthplaces are correctly marked 'indirect' as hearsay. Calculated birth year is correctly marked 'indirect.' However, Patrick's birthplace (Ireland) is incorrectly marked as 'direct' evidence when it should be 'indirect'—Mary Flynn reported it secondhand, not from personal witness. The guidance explicitly states birth-related facts from the informant who was not present are indirect evidence. This single misclassification is the main error; otherwise evidence type assignments align with source content and proximity.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Correctness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All 17 assertions extracted from the marriage record are factually accurate. Informants correctly identified (groom Patrick, bride Catherine, parents named as stated, Rev. O'Connor as officiant). Evidence types properly distinguished: direct for stated facts (names, ages, birthplace, occupations, marriage date/place), indirect for computed birth years. Assertion values match source exactly. The skill correctly identified the potential birthplace conflict (Ireland vs. Pennsylvania in project objective) and noted it appropriately.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Extracted comprehensive assertions for all parties present on the record: groom Patrick Flynn (7 assertions), bride Catherine Gallagher (6 assertions), fathers and mothers of both (4 assertions). Covered all key facts: names, ages, birth years, birthplaces, residences, occupations, parents' names, and marriage event details. Witnesses noted as FAN associates with identifying names. No required facts omitted; the record's content is fully captured.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All 4 MCP tool calls executed successfully with appropriate arguments. research_log_append recorded the user-provided record correctly with matching fields. tree_edit added the source to GedcomX with correct operation. Second research_append batch successfully persisted 17 assertions and source metadata after source was created. Arguments match the fixture expectations and fixtures matched (kind: 'live'). No fixture_not_found errors.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Each assertion is a single extractable fact. Groom's name, age, birth year, birthplace, residence, and occupation are separate assertions with distinct fact_types. Parents' names are separate from groom's record. Marriage event (date and place combined in value field) is appropriate as a single marriage fact_type. Compound information properly decomposed—no instance where multiple distinct facts are crammed into one assertion.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Informant identification
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Informants clearly identified throughout. For groom's facts (age, birthplace, occupation, parents' names): informant = 'Patrick Flynn (groom)' with proximity 'self', correctly reflecting that the groom provided this information. For bride's facts: informant = 'Catherine Gallagher (bride)' with proximity 'self'. For marriage event (date, place): informant = 'Rev. Daniel O'Connor' with proximity 'official_duty', appropriately recognizing the officiant as witness to the event. Recorder (clerk) role understood as separate from informant role.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Evidence types correctly assigned: direct evidence for all stated facts (names, ages, birthplaces, residences, occupations, marriage date/place—all explicitly written on the record). Indirect evidence for computed birth years (~1845, ~1848)—correctly marked as 'estimated' with explanation that years are derived from stated age minus marriage year, not explicitly stated. Marriage date and place marked direct because explicitly stated on license. No evidence types mis-assigned; classifications support reliable downstream conflict resolution.

output.text_response:
Now I have the project state. Let me note the key context:
- **Project objective:** Identify the parents of Patrick Flynn (I1)
- **Existing sources:** none — this will be `src_001`
- **Tree:** Patrick Flynn (I1) only

Roles in this record: `groom` (Patrick Flynn), `bride` (Catherine Gallagher), `father_of_groom` (Thomas Flynn), `mother_of_groom` (Mary Brennan), `father_of_bride` (James Gallagher), `mother_of_bride` (Bridget Dolan).

No sibling stubs needed — Patrick is the `groom`, not a `child_N` role.

**Step 4 — log the user-provided record:****Step 5a/5b — batch source + 17 assertions:**The source description must exist in `tree.gedcomx.json` before the source entry references it. I'll add `S1` to the tree first, then re-run the batch.

**Step 5c — add source description to tree:**`S1` confirmed. Now the full batch:All 18 ops wrote successfully. Here's the summary:

---

**Source:** `src_001` → `S1` — Schuylkill Co., PA Marriage License & Certificate no. 4872 (original)

**Assertions persisted — 17 total (a_001–a_017):**
| Role | Count | Facts |
|---|---|---|
| `groom` | 7 | name, age, birth year (indirect), birthplace, residence, occupation, marriage |
| `bride` | 6 | name, age, birth year (indirect), birthplace, residence, marriage |
| `father_of_groom` | 1 | name: Thomas Flynn |
| `mother_of_groom` | 1 | name: Mary Brennan |
| `father_of_bride` | 1 | name: James Gallagher |
| `mother_of_bride` | 1 | name: Bridget Dolan |

**Tree changes:** `S1` added; no person stubs (groom is not a `child_N` role).

**Key findings:**
- **Directly answers the research objective** — Patrick Flynn names his parents as **Thomas Flynn** (father) and **Mary Brennan** (mother); Thomas Flynn signed as witness_1, corroborating his presence.
- ⚠️ **Birthplace conflict** — groom states birth in **Ireland**; project description says "born in Pennsylvania." This may indicate a data error in the project objective, or this is a different Patrick Flynn. Needs corroboration before linking to I1.
- Patrick's age 25 → birth ~1845 is consistent with the project's "ca. 1845."

**Next steps:** Run `check-warnings`, then `person-evidence` to evaluate whether this groom links to I1 (resolving the birthplace conflict is the key question).

---

**Source:** `src_010` → `S9` ("1870 U.S. Federal Census"), derivative, FamilySearch, accessed 2026-07-04
**Log:** `log_006` (nil result, user-provided)
**Assertion:** `a_014` — `record_role: absent`, `evidence_type: negative`, linked to `q_001`

**Key finding:** Patrick Flynn is absent from the 1870 Schuylkill County census index, despite confirmed enumeration there in 1850 and 1860. Four alternative explanations noted in `informant_bias_notes`: relocation, enumerator miss, indexing/spelling error, damaged pages.

**Source classified `derivative`** — the search was through an index, not a browse of the original images. The original schedules were not independently reviewed, so the absence is from the index only; a page-by-page browse of NARA M593 for Schuylkill County would provide stronger negative evidence.

**Next steps:** Browse the original 1870 census images for Schuylkill County to confirm vs. index error; also search 1870 census for adjacent counties (Northumberland, Carbon, Dauphin) in case Patrick had relocated — then continue with the 1880 and 1900 censuses to re-locate him.

warnings.advisory: Skill called MCP tools but the rubric has no dimension name suggesting tool-usage coverage (matched against keywords: ['tool usage', 'tool use', 'tool work', 'tool call', 'argument quality', 'argument-quality', 'response interpretation', 'tool selection', 'mcp tool', 'fixture']). The judge will grade other dimensions but won't score tool work explicitly. Consider adding a tool-usage dimension or renaming an existing one.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: The skill correctly identified and recorded Patrick Flynn's absence from the 1870 census as negative evidence. The assertion accurately captures the search parameters (Schuylkill County, age ~25, birth ~1845), source classification (derivative—index search, not primary), and appropriate alternatives for the non-appearance. Log entry, source, and assertion are factually sound and properly linked to question q_001 (parentage research).

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill addressed all elements requested: created a new 1870 census source (src_010/S9), added a log entry (log_006), and recorded the negative-evidence assertion (a_014) with record_role: absent. The citation details (who, what, when, where) are complete. Four alternative explanations for the absence are documented in informant_bias_notes, demonstrating thorough coverage of the search gap.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: All MCP tool calls were well-formed and semantically correct. The research_log_append call properly captured user_provided tool, outcome 'negative', and detailed query fields (surname, given, birth_year, jurisdiction, collection). The tree_edit call correctly added source S9. The final research_append batch correctly appended both source (with S9 gedcomx_source_description_id) and assertion (with source_id src_010). No fixture_not_found errors; the second research_append attempt failed validation but was corrected by pre-adding S9 to tree.gedcomx.json.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: The single assertion (a_014) is atomic: it extracts one fact—Patrick Flynn's absence from the 1870 residence—with a single fact_type (residence) and record_role (absent). No compound claims are bundled; the assertion is cleanly decomposed and queryable by downstream skills seeking residence evidence or negative evidence.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: The informant is correctly identified as 'Researcher (analyst)' rather than the enumerator or a household member. The informant_proximity is set to 'unknown' (appropriate for a researcher-conducted index search), and the informant_bias_notes explicitly clarify that the absence is an analyst inference, not a fact reported by a record informant. This distinction is crucial for negative evidence and is handled properly.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Evidence type is correctly assigned as 'negative'—the finding is the absence of Patrick Flynn in the index despite expected enumeration. The record_role 'absent' and negative evidence_type correctly reflect that this is a deliberately-searched-for-but-not-found result. The source_classification 'derivative' accurately notes the index search rather than page-by-page primary image review, appropriately qualifying the strength of the negative evidence.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Correctness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill correctly declined to perform record-extraction and instead routed the user to search-records. This is the correct behavior for a negative test case where the user is asking the skill to search for a record rather than extract from an existing one. The decision shows sound judgment in understanding the skill's scope.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill fully addressed the task by identifying that this is a search request (not a record-extraction task) and routing the user appropriately to the search-records skill. The response was complete in its decline and redirection, covering what was needed.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: No MCP tool calls were made by the skill. This is expected behavior for a negative test where the skill correctly declined to use its own tools and instead routed the user to a different skill. N/A applies here.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: No MCP tool calls were made by the skill. This is expected behavior for a negative test where the skill correctly declined to use its own tools and instead routed the user to a different skill. N/A applies here.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Correctness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All assertions extracted are factually correct and well-sourced from the provided 1850 census record. The skill correctly identified Patrick Flynn's name, approximate age (5), birth year (1845), birthplace (Ireland), residence, and relationship to Thomas Flynn. The skill also correctly noted the conflict between the census birthplace (Ireland) and project objective (Pennsylvania), flagging it appropriately. The FamilySearch match check (LZNY-BRF) was correctly reported as already accepted in the tree. No fabricated material or contradictions.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill addressed all aspects required by the user message: extracted all meaningful assertions from the record (name, age, birth year, birthplace, residence for Patrick; name and residence for Thomas), added the source to tree.gedcomx.json and research.json, created appropriate log entry, and checked the FamilySearch match status for persona MXHY-TP4 as instructed. All 8 assertions were extracted and persisted.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Most tool calls matched expected arguments correctly: record_person_matches call used correct id 'MXHY-TP4'; tree_edit and research_append calls successfully persisted data. However, the second research_append call (with all 8 assertions) initially failed validation—it was executed after tree_edit corrected the source reference issue, then retried with record_persona_id set to null instead of P1. While the skill recovered and succeeded on retry, the initial failure and the need to nullify record_persona_id (per the test context expecting P1) indicates a meaningful misstep in argument construction.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Each of the 8 assertions is atomic and represents a single extractable fact. Birth year (a_003) is separate from birthplace (a_004); age (a_002) is distinct from both. The relationship assertion (a_006) is singular: 'child of Thomas Flynn' without compounding inference method into the value. Name and residence assertions for both Patrick and Thomas are properly isolated. Downstream skills can query individual fact_type values without parsing compound claims.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Informant identification
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: For Patrick's name, age, birth year, and birthplace: 'unknown household member (likely a parent)' with proximity 'household_member' and explicit informant_bias_notes explaining why a parent likely reported (child age ~5 could not self-report). For Thomas's name: 'unknown household member (likely self or spouse)' with reasoning that adults typically self-reported. For both residences: 'census enumerator' with proximity 'witness' since the enumerator observed location. Informant fields distinguish recorder from actual reporter, and proximity field correctly applies 'household_member' for facts requiring household knowledge.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Evidence types correctly assigned: Patrick's name, age, birthplace, and residence marked 'direct' (facts explicitly on the record). Birth year marked 'indirect' with clear reasoning: computed by subtracting indexed age from census year. Relationship marked 'indirect' with explicit note that 1850 census has no relationship column and position was inferred by FamilySearch indexer. The skill explicitly states 'Pre-1880 census relationships are always indirect,' showing correct understanding of historical census structure. No misclassifications.

output.text_response:
Reading both files first, then I'll execute.

**Current state:**
- `research.json`: no sources, assertions, or log entries yet → this source will be `src_001`
- `tree.gedcomx.json`: one person, I1 (Patrick Flynn); no Thomas Flynn → sibling-stub trigger **does not fire** (no in-tree parent found)

**Sibling / parent enumeration:**
- Parents in tree: Thomas Flynn (head_of_household) → NOT in tree.gedcomx.json → skip stubs
- Siblings: none (only one child on this record)

**Identified roles & planned assertions:**
- `child_1` (Patrick, P1): name, age ~5 (direct), birth year ~1845 (indirect), birthplace Ireland, residence, relationship (indirect/inferred)
- `head_of_household` (Thomas, P2): name, residence

**Note:** Record states Patrick's birthplace as **Ireland**, but project objective says "born ca. 1845 in Pennsylvania" — flagging for conflict resolution after extraction.

Creating the log entry first:Log entry `log_001` created. Now batching source + all 8 assertions:Two issues to fix:
1. `S1` must exist in `tree.gedcomx.json` before `research_append` validates the cross-reference.
2. No sidecar exists for `log_001` (no `stagedResultsRef` was passed from search-records), so `record_persona_id` must be `null` on all assertions.

Writing the tree source first:`S1` written. Now batching source + 8 assertions with `record_persona_id: null`:All persisted. Now checking FamilySearch match status for persona P1 (MXHY-TP4):All done. Here's the terse summary:

---

**Extraction complete — 1850 U.S. Census, MXHY-TP4**

- **Source:** `src_001` / `S1` — original, FamilySearch, 1850 U.S. Federal Census
- **Assertions:** 8 total
  - `child_1` (Patrick Flynn, P1): a_001 name · a_002 age ~5 (direct) · a_003 birth yr ~1845 (indirect) · a_004 birthplace Ireland (direct) · a_005 residence Branch Twp, Schuylkill, PA · a_006 relationship child_inferred
  - `head_of_household` (Thomas Flynn, P2): a_007 name · a_008 residence Branch Twp, Schuylkill, PA
- **Tree changes:** `S1` added; no sibling stubs (Thomas Flynn not yet in tree)
- **⚠ Conflict flag:** Record states Patrick born in **Ireland**; project objective says "born in Pennsylvania" — resolve via conflict-resolution or additional records
- **Match check:** Persona MXHY-TP4 is **already accepted** in FamilySearch tree as person **LZNY-BRF** (Patrick Flynn, confidence 5, score 0.97) — worth noting when running person-evidence for I1

**Next steps:** `check-warnings` → `person-evidence` to link a_001–a_006 to I1 (and decide whether LZNY-BRF informs the local tree), then address the Ireland vs. Pennsylvania birthplace conflict.

args.notes: Record MXHY-TP4: Patrick Flynn, child in Thomas Flynn household, Branch Township, Schuylkill, PA. Birthplace indexed as Ireland.

**Next steps:** Run `person-evidence` to link P2/Thomas and P3/Mary to new tree persons, establish the ParentChild edges, then `check-warnings` for plausibility checks.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted 15 assertions atomically and correctly identified all three persons (P1, P2, P3) from the 1850 census with appropriate evidence types and informant designations. However, the first MCP tool call (research_append) failed with a validation error: 'gedcomx_source_description_id S1 not found in tree.gedcomx.json sources,' indicating the skill attempted to write sources before the tree source was created. The skill recovered by calling tree_edit first, then reissuing research_append successfully. All factual content in the final assertions is accurate—relationships correctly marked indirect, informants properly reasoned, and record_id carried verbatim as the full ARK URL. The recovery pattern shows the assertions themselves are sound, though the initial workflow ordering was incorrect.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted all extractable facts from the record: for Patrick (P1) — name, age, birth year (inferred), birthplace, residence, and two relationship assertions; for Thomas (P2) — name, gender, residence, and parent relationship; for Mary (P3) — name, gender, residence, and parent relationship. All three persons in the gedcomx were covered, all facts from the record were decomposed into separate assertions, and the skill correctly referenced log_001 (the pre-existing search-records log entry) without creating a duplicate. No silent omissions of factual content.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: The skill made three MCP calls: (1) research_append with sources/assertions before tree_edit — this call failed validation (not a fixture_not_found, but a correctness error showing reversed order); (2) tree_edit to add_source — correctly invoked with expected title and URL, returned S1; (3) research_append again with sources and all 15 assertions — succeeded. The first call's failure was an ordering mistake, not a fixture error. The second and third calls' args matched expectations. Tool arguments themselves were well-formed (full ARK URLs, correct persona IDs, proper log_entry_id references). The sequence error (trying to append sources before the source existed in tree.gedcomx) shows a control-flow issue rather than malformed arguments.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Each of the 15 assertions represents a single extractable fact. Name is one assertion; age is separate; birth year (inferred from age) is separate; birthplace is separate; residence is separate; each relationship (Patrick→Thomas, Patrick→Mary, Thomas→Patrick, Mary→Patrick) is its own assertion. For all three persons, facts were decomposed correctly: gender (for P2, P3), names, residences, relationships each occupy their own assertion with a single fact_type. No compound claims were bundled; downstream skills can query individual facts independently.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: For biographical facts (name, age, birthplace) on Patrick (a 5-year-old), the skill correctly identified 'unknown household member (likely a parent)' with proximity 'household_member' and detailed reasoning in informant_bias_notes explaining that a young child could not self-report. For Thomas and Mary's names, 'unknown household member (likely self or spouse)' with appropriate proximity reasoning. For residence and gender facts across all persons, 'census enumerator' with proximity 'witness' is documented. Each informant distinction properly separates the recorder (enumerator) from the actual reporter (household member), with clear rationales tied to the 1850 census context.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: All evidence types are correctly assigned per the 1850 census source. Direct evidence: name (stated in record), age (stated in age column), birthplace (stated in birthplace column), gender (stated in sex column), residence (enumerator-recorded location). Indirect evidence: birth year (inferred by arithmetic: 1850 − 5 = ~1845, not directly stated), and both ParentChild relationships (no explicit relationship column in 1850; inferred from household position and indexer association). The skill's notes explicitly reference that 1850 has no relationship column, confirming the indirect classification is grounded in source structure. No mismatches between evidence type and source content.
